title: Indexing & Slicing
summary: Learn how to grab any character or chunk from a string using Python's powerful indexing and slicing syntax.

blocks.md: Imagine a string as a row of numbered seats in a cinema. Every character has its own seat number — a **position** — and you can reach into any seat to grab what's sitting there. This is called **indexing**. You can also say "give me seats 2 through 5" to grab a whole section at once. That's called **slicing**. Both skills are incredibly useful any time you work with text.

Python numbers positions starting from **0**, not 1. So the very first character is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on. This surprises almost every beginner — but you'll get used to it quickly.

blocks.code:
word = "Python"

print(word[0])  # first character
print(word[1])  # second character
print(word[5])  # sixth (last) character
blocks.caption: Square brackets with a number pull out one character.

blocks.title: The Elevator Analogy
blocks.md: In many countries, the ground floor of a building is called **floor 0**, not floor 1. Python counts characters the same way — the ground floor is 0. Once that clicks, everything else follows naturally.

blocks.text: Negative Indexes Count from the End

blocks.md: Python also lets you count **backwards** from the end using negative numbers. `-1` is always the last character, `-2` is second-to-last, and so on. This is handy when you don't know how long a string is.

blocks.code:
word = "Python"

print(word[-1])  # last character
print(word[-2])  # second-to-last
print(word[-6])  # same as word[0]
blocks.caption: Negative indexes let you count from the right.

blocks.title: Index Out of Range
blocks.md:
If you use an index that doesn't exist — like `word[99]` on a 6-letter string — Python raises an **IndexError** and your program crashes. Always make sure your index is within bounds!

```python
word = "Python"
print(word[99])  # IndexError: string index out of range
```

Instead of one seat, a **slice** grabs a whole range of characters. The syntax is `s[start:stop]`. Python returns the characters from position `start` up to — but **not including** — position `stop`. The `stop` being exclusive is the trickiest part, so read that again slowly.

blocks.title: A Quick Way to Remember
blocks.md: Think of the numbers as **gaps between characters**, not the characters themselves. Position 0 is the gap before `P`, position 1 is the gap before `y`, etc. `word[0:3]` means "cut at gap 0 and gap 3" — everything between those cuts is returned.
You can leave out `start` or `stop` and Python will fill in a sensible default. Omitting `start` means "begin at the very beginning". Omitting `stop` means "go all the way to the end".

blocks.code:
word = "Python"

print(word[:3])   # from start up to (not including) position 3
print(word[2:])   # from position 2 to the end
print(word[:])    # the whole string — a full copy
blocks.caption: Omit start or stop to slice from/to the ends of the string.

blocks.md: Slices have a third optional value: `s[start:stop:step]`. The **step** controls how many positions to jump between each character you pick. The default step is 1 (take every character). A step of 2 takes every other character.

blocks.code:
word = "Python"

print(word[0:6:2])  # every 2nd character
print(word[::3])    # every 3rd character from start to end
blocks.caption: The third number in a slice is the step size.

blocks.md: A step of **-1** walks backwards through the string. Combined with omitted start and stop, `s[::-1]` is the classic Python one-liner for reversing a string.

blocks.caption: [::-1] is the Pythonic way to reverse any string.

blocks.title: Slices Never Crash
blocks.md:
Unlike single-index access, **slices never raise an IndexError**. If your start or stop is out of bounds, Python just returns whatever it can.

```python
word = "Hi"
print(word[0:100])  # no crash — returns 'Hi'
print(word[50:])    # no crash — returns ''
```

This makes slicing much safer for situations where you're not sure of the length.

Everything you just learned transfers directly to **lists**. Lists use the exact same `[index]` and `[start:stop:step]` syntax. So once you're comfortable slicing strings, you already know how to slice lists — which you'll see in a later lesson.

blocks.code:
colors = ["red", "green", "blue", "yellow", "purple"]

print(colors[0])     # first item
print(colors[-1])    # last item
print(colors[1:3])   # a slice of the list
print(colors[::-1])  # reversed list
blocks.caption: Slicing syntax is identical for lists — a big time-saver!

blocks.question: What does `"abcde"[1:4]` return?
blocks.explanation: Slicing starts at index 1 (the character 'b') and goes up to — but not including — index 4 (the character 'e'). So you get positions 1, 2, and 3, which are 'b', 'c', 'd' — giving the string "bcd".

keyTakeaways: Python indexes start at 0 — the first character is always at position 0.
Negative indexes count from the end: -1 is the last character.
Slicing with s[start:stop] returns characters from start up to (not including) stop.
Omit start or stop to slice from the beginning or to the end; add a step with s[start:stop:step].
s[::-1] reverses any string, and slices never raise IndexError unlike single-index access.

exercise.prompt:
You're given the string `sentence = "The quick brown fox"`. Using only indexing and slicing (no other string methods), do the following:
1. Print the first character.
2. Print the last character.
3. Print the first three characters.
4. Print the word `"fox"` (the last 3 characters).
5. Print the whole sentence reversed.
exercise.starterCode:
sentence = "The quick brown fox"

# 1. First character

# 2. Last character

# 3. First three characters

# 4. Last three characters ("fox")

# 5. Reversed sentence
exercise.hint: Remember: negative indexes count from the end, and s[::-1] reverses. The last 3 characters can be grabbed with s[-3:].
exercise.solution:
sentence = "The quick brown fox"

# 1. First character
print(sentence[0])

# 2. Last character
print(sentence[-1])

# 3. First three characters
print(sentence[:3])

# 4. Last three characters ("fox")
print(sentence[-3:])

# 5. Reversed sentence
print(sentence[::-1])
